_comment: Beach configuration for Northern Beaches Surf Check. Each beach has a primary aspect (direction the beach faces in degrees from North) plus left and right offsets defining the direct swell window. Swell arriving within the left/right window is considered fully exposed; beyond it, power falls off due to shadowing.

beaches.name: Long Reef
beaches.notes: Northernmost beach. Wide exposure from E (90°) to S (180°). Mostly open ocean with only minor headland sheltering to the north — picks up a broad range of swell directions.

beaches.name: Dee Why
beaches.notes: Southeast facing beach, sheltered by southern headland. Wide left exposure from ENE (67.5°) to primary, but tightly shadowed on the right (only to SSE at 157.5°). Best with SE or ESE swell — loses south swells quickly.

beaches.name: Curl Curl
beaches.notes: East-southeast exposed beach with consistent swell. Nearly symmetrical window from ENE (67.5°) to SSE (157.5°). Good all-rounder — picks up most swell from east through south.

beaches.name: Freshwater
beaches.notes: Southeast facing beach, protected by northern headland. Moderate left exposure to ESE (100°) but tightly shadowed on the right beyond SSE (157.5°). Catches east and southeast swells but loses southerly swell quickly.

beaches.name: North Steyne
beaches.notes: Eastern end of Manly Beach, exposed to east swells. Symmetrical window from NE (45°) to SE (135°). Wide open to any swell with an easterly component — the default north-end beach when there's east swell.

beaches.name: South Steyne
beaches.notes: East-northeast end of Manly Beach, NE swell exposure. Window from NNE (22.5°) to E (90°). Tight right shadow — loses south swells quickly. Best with northerly or north-easterly swell.
